hero.headline: How does Montana state income tax work for employers in 2026?
hero.subhead: Two brackets to 5.65%, $47,300 UI wage base, and the WDEA: the only US state that isn't at-will after probation.
hero.partner_badge: Served by Teamed partner network (US payroll partner)

answer_capsule: Montana state income tax uses a 2-bracket system in 2026: 4.7% up to $47,500 for single filers and 5.65% above — the top rate fell from 5.9% under HB 337 (2025 Legislature), effective 1st January 2026. Unemployment insurance runs on a $47,300 wage base with rates from 0% to 6.12%; over 7,100 employers paid zero in 2026 under HB 210's trust-fund trigger. Montana is the only US state where at-will employment does not apply after probation — the Wrongful Discharge from Employment Act (WDEA, MCA 39-2-901) requires good cause for termination. No county or local income tax. Teamed runs Montana payroll at $599 per employee per month, Zero FX.

sections.h2: What is Montana's state income tax rate in 2026?
sections.answer_first: Montana runs on two income tax brackets in 2026: 4.7% up to $47,500 for single filers, and 5.65% on income above that. The top rate dropped from 5.9% under HB 337, effective 1st January 2026.
sections.body:
HB 337, passed in the 2025 Montana Legislative Session, restructured the state's income tax brackets. For single filers and those filing as married-filing-separately, the 4.7% rate applies to taxable income from $1 to $47,500; the 5.65% rate applies above that. For joint filers, the 4.7% bracket covers up to $95,000; for head-of-household filers, up to $71,250. There's no county or local income tax layered on top — Montana is a single-rate-at-state-level state.

The withholding method changed alongside the bracket reform. The 2026 Montana MW-4 form now relies on the federal standard deduction by filing status as the base deduction, aligning the state calculation with federal practice. Existing employees should update their MW-4 with the new form, particularly if life circumstances have changed. Employers register for withholding via the Montana Taxpayer Access Point (TAP); your Withholding Account Number arrives in 10 to 14 days.

Looking further ahead: HB 337 schedules another rate reduction for 2027, when the top rate drops to 5.4% and the lower bracket threshold for single filers rises to $65,000. Build that into your multi-year payroll projections. Source: Montana DOR, HB 337 resource hub (revenue.mt.gov/news/recent-news/HB-337).

sections.h2: What is Montana's unemployment insurance tax rate in 2026?
sections.answer_first: Montana UI rates run 0.00% to 6.12% in 2026 on a taxable wage base of $47,300 per employee. New employers typically pay around 1.18%. Over 7,100 employers paid zero under HB 210's trust-fund reduction trigger.
sections.body:
Montana sets the UI taxable wage base annually at 80% of the average annual wage from the preceding calendar year, rounded to the nearest $100. For 2026 that figure is $47,300 — one of the higher state wage bases in the Mountain West, which means the exposure window is wider than states like Maryland ($8,500) or Arizona ($8,000).

The rate schedule assigns employers to one of 12 tiers (Schedule I through XII) based on the ratio of Montana's UI Trust Fund balance to total covered wages as of 31st October each year. HB 210, enacted in the 2025 session, added an automatic rate-reduction trigger: when the fund exceeds 2.8% of total covered wages, all experience-rated employers receive a 0.20 percentage point reduction. That triggered for 2026, cutting rates for 32,873 employers and pushing over 7,100 to a zero rate. Montana reported $23 million in aggregate UI tax savings for businesses.

Deficit employers (those whose accounts have more claims charged than contributions paid) face rates between 2.72% and 6.12%. New employers without a claims history typically enter at around 1.18%; the exact rate varies by industry. Quarterly UI reports and payments are filed with the Montana UI Division (uid.dli.mt.gov). The maximum annual UI exposure per employee at the ceiling rate is $2,893 (6.12% × $47,300) — about ten times higher than Maryland's maximum, so the wage base matters for your cost model.

sections.h2: How do employers register and remit Montana state income tax withholding?
sections.answer_first: Montana employers register via the Montana Taxpayer Access Point (TAP) and receive a Withholding Account Number within 10 to 14 days. Remittance frequency — quarterly, monthly, or accelerated — scales with your withholding volume.
sections.body:
The 2026 Montana MW-4 form is the employee's withholding certificate. Every new hire submits one before their first paycheck. The new form method uses the federal standard deduction by filing status as the base deduction — employees no longer need to compute Montana-specific allowances separately. Without a completed MW-4 on file, you withhold at the maximum rate.

Montana has a reciprocal agreement only with North Dakota: Montana residents working in North Dakota (and vice versa) are exempt from withholding in the work state. For all other cross-border employees, follow the standard Montana withholding rules based on wages earned in or attributable to Montana.

For annual reconciliation, use Form MW-3. Electronic filing is required for employers filing 25 or more W-2 forms. For payroll details outside the scope of the MW-4 tables — such as supplemental wages, non-cash compensation, or employee stock — consult Montana Publication 1 (Montana Guide to Wage Withholding), updated for 2026. Source: revenue.mt.gov/news/recent-news/2026-withholding-updates.

A named Teamed payroll specialist handles the full Montana withholding cycle for every employee on the EOR arrangement: MW-4 collection, withholding calculation, remittance timing, and annual reconciliation. One platform, one invoice, compliance monitored automatically. If you want a structured advisory review of your Montana setup, that's included.

sections.h2: What is the Montana Wrongful Discharge from Employment Act?
sections.answer_first: Montana is the only US state where at-will employment does not apply after the probationary period. The Wrongful Discharge from Employment Act (WDEA, MCA 39-2-901) requires employers to have 'good cause' to terminate an employee who has completed probation.
sections.body:
The WDEA, enacted in 1987 and codified at MCA Title 39, Chapter 2, Part 9, distinguishes Montana from every other US state. During the probationary period — which defaults to 12 months from hire and can be extended by the employer to a maximum of 18 months — employment is at-will. After probation, 'good cause' is required.

'Good cause' under MCA 39-2-903 means reasonable job-related grounds for dismissal: failure to satisfactorily perform job duties, disruption of the employer's operation, or another legitimate business reason. The employer bears the burden. Remedies for wrongful discharge include lost wages and fringe benefits for up to four years from the discharge date, plus interest. The WDEA preempts most common-law tort claims (MCA 39-2-913), capping the employee's recovery — but that cap is still a four-year wage recovery bill.

For employers, three things matter. First: write a probationary period policy and put it in the offer letter and handbook. Montana courts treat handbook provisions as legally binding. Second: document performance issues while they exist — verbal warnings, written PIPs, and outcome notes all constitute good-cause evidence before a termination becomes necessary. Third: if your Montana worker is classified as an independent contractor, require an Independent Contractor Exemption Certificate (ICEC) from the Montana DLI; without one, misclassification risk is elevated precisely because the WDEA makes employee status carry a higher legal exposure than most states. Source: MCA 39-2-901 through 39-2-915 (mca.legmt.gov).

polaroid_data.body_line: At-will employment ends after probation. Good cause required under WDEA (MCA 39-2-901).
polaroid_data.caption: Good cause or go home.

sections.h2: Does Montana have a local or county income tax?
sections.answer_first: No. Montana has no county or local income tax. You withhold at the state rate only, from a single Montana Withholding Account Number. Montana has a reciprocal agreement with North Dakota, and no other state.
sections.body:
Montana is one of a small set of US states with genuinely simple payroll tax structure: one withholding account, one set of tables, one remittance destination. There is no municipality, county, or school district income tax layered on top of state income tax. Unlike Maryland (24 county rates), Ohio (hundreds of local rates), or New York City (separate city income tax), your Montana payroll calculation produces a single combined state tax figure.

The North Dakota reciprocity agreement means Montana residents working in North Dakota do not need North Dakota state income tax withheld (and vice versa). For remote employees working in Montana but residing in another state, apply normal cross-border apportionment principles — Montana taxes wages earned in or attributable to Montana, while the employee's home state typically allows a credit for taxes paid to Montana.

The practical effect: Montana payroll compliance is low-complexity for income tax purposes. The complexity sits in the WDEA (termination) and UI (higher wage base than most states) layers — not the withholding setup.

sections.h2: What do out-of-state companies need to know before hiring in Montana?
sections.answer_first: Montana's minimum wage is $10.85/hour as of 1st January 2026. The state has no mandatory paid sick leave, no state paid family leave programme, and no local income tax. The WDEA is the variable most employers underestimate.
sections.body:
Three things that catch first-time Montana employers off guard. First, the WDEA. Most US employment law discussions treat at-will employment as universal; Montana is the exception. If you hire without a written probationary period policy, WDEA protection may attach from day one. The written policy — in the offer letter or handbook — is the lever. Second, the UI wage base. At $47,300, Montana's wage base is higher than many states. For a $60,000 salary, you're paying UI on 79% of the annual wage. Build it into your cost model from the start. Third, contractor classification. Montana requires an Independent Contractor Exemption Certificate (ICEC) from the Montana DLI for contractors. If an ICEC isn't on file and a reclassification audit occurs, WDEA exposure multiplies the retroactive liability.

Montana has no state disability insurance tax and no SDI surcharge. Workers' compensation is required for most employers, administered through the Montana State Fund or approved private insurers. The minimum wage adjusts annually by CPI (announced by 30th September, effective 1st January). No tip credit and no training wage are permitted. Federal FMLA applies at 50 or more employees within 75 miles; Montana has no state FMLA extension for smaller employers.

Teamed's in-house legal specialist supports WDEA-compliant offer letter and handbook drafting for every Montana hire. Your real human contact at Teamed — not a portal, not a chatbot — fields questions about ICEC requirements, probationary period policy, and UI rate notices. One platform, one invoice, all of Montana's compliance layers on one workflow.

faq.q: What is Montana's state income tax rate in 2026?
faq.a: Montana has two income tax brackets in 2026. The 4.7% rate applies to taxable income up to $47,500 for single filers (or $95,000 for married filing jointly). The 5.65% rate applies above those thresholds. The top rate fell from 5.9% under HB 337, effective 1st January 2026. There is no county or local income tax.

faq.q: What is the Montana unemployment insurance taxable wage base in 2026?
faq.a: The Montana UI taxable wage base is $47,300 in 2026, set at 80% of the prior year's average annual wage. UI rates range from 0.00% to 6.12%. Over 7,100 employers paid zero in 2026 under HB 210's trust-fund reduction trigger. New employers typically enter at approximately 1.18%.

faq.q: Is Montana an at-will employment state?
faq.a: Montana is the only US state that is not fully at-will after probation. The Wrongful Discharge from Employment Act (WDEA, MCA 39-2-901) requires 'good cause' for termination once the probationary period — defaulting to 12 months, maximum 18 months — is complete. During probation, employment is at-will.

faq.q: Does Montana have a local income tax?
faq.a: No. Montana has no county or local income tax. Employers withhold only state income tax using the Montana Taxpayer Access Point (TAP). Montana has a reciprocal withholding agreement with North Dakota, and no other state.
